units.title: Container Element (div)
units.summary: The HTML div element is used to define a container that groups other HTML elements together.
units.source_span:
The HTML `div` element defines a container. ```html

Tourism

Plan your trip wherever you want to go

```

explanation.text: A `div` is an HTML element that acts as a container—a box that holds and groups other HTML elements together. You use it to organize related content on a webpage, like grouping a heading, text, and a button into one section.

analogy.text: A div is like a cardboard box that holds different items together. Just as the box itself is the container and the items inside (a book, a toy, a pen) are the separate things being grouped, the div element is the container and the h1, p, and button inside it are the separate HTML elements being grouped together.
analogy.grounding_check: Box = 'div element defines a container'; items inside (book, toy, pen) = 'other HTML elements' (h1, p, button in source example)

scenarios.text: You're building a tourism website and want to group a heading, description, and button together as one promotional section. You wrap all three inside a `div` so they stay together as a unit.

code_playground.html:
Tourism

Plan your trip wherever you want to go

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the HTML div element?
options: To define a container that groups other HTML elements together
To create a line break between elements
To add styling directly to text
To embed images on a page
questions.explanation: The source states that the div element 'defines a container' that groups other HTML elements together. The other options describe different HTML elements or features, not the div's purpose.

questions.question: In the example, what elements are grouped inside the div container?
options: An h1 heading, a paragraph, and a button
Only a paragraph and a button
A div, an h1, and a link
questions.explanation: The source example shows an h1, a p, and a button nested inside the div tags. The other options either omit elements or include elements not present in the example.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid way to use a div element?
options: Wrapping multiple elements like headings, paragraphs, and buttons inside it
Using it instead of a paragraph tag to display text
Placing it only around single words
Nesting divs is not allowed in HTML
questions.explanation: The source example demonstrates that a div groups multiple different types of elements (h1, p, button) together, which is its valid use. The other options either misrepresent the div's function or state false restrictions.

questions.question: What does the opening tag `<div>` do?
options: Marks the start of a container that groups elements
Creates a new paragraph on the page
questions.explanation:
The opening `
` tag begins the container, as shown in the source example where it appears before the grouped elements. The closing tag `
` ends it. The other options describe different HTML tags or the wrong tag function.

units.title: CSS Rule Syntax
units.summary: CSS rules consist of a selector followed by a block containing one or more property-value pairs.

explanation.text: A CSS rule is a block of code that styles elements on a web page. It has two main parts: a selector (which picks which elements to style) and a declaration block (the curly braces containing one or more property-value pairs). Each property-value pair tells the browser what style to apply—for example, `color: blue;` makes text blue.

analogy.text: A CSS rule is like a mailing address label on a package. The selector (like 'h1') is the address that identifies which package to label, just as the selector identifies which HTML element to style. The curly braces contain property-value pairs (like 'color: blue;'), just like the label contains multiple pieces of information—each property is one line of the address, and each value is the specific detail for that line.
analogy.grounding_check: selector → 'selector' in source; property-value pairs → 'property1: value1; property2: value2' in source; block structure → 'block containing one or more property-value pairs' in source

scenarios.text: You want to make all paragraph text red and larger. You write a CSS rule with `p` as the selector and two property-value pairs inside the braces: `color: red;` and `font-size: 18px;`.

code_playground.html:
This paragraph is red and larger.

This one too.

questions.question: What two main parts make up a CSS rule?
options: A selector and a declaration block
An HTML element and a style
A class name and a color
questions.explanation: The source shows a CSS rule has a selector (like the word before the curly braces) followed by a block in curly braces containing properties and values. The other options describe parts within a rule or confuse CSS with HTML, but not the two main parts of the rule itself.

questions.question: In a CSS rule, what goes inside the curly braces?
questions.explanation: The source explicitly states the block contains 'one or more property-value pairs' separated by semicolons. The other options either describe the selector (which goes outside the braces) or are too narrow.

questions.question: What character ends each property-value pair in a CSS rule?
questions.explanation: The source shows each property-value pair ends with a semicolon (e.g., `property1: value1;`). A colon separates the property from its value, not the pair itself; commas and periods are not used in this context.

questions.question: How many property-value pairs must a CSS declaration block contain?
questions.explanation: The source states the block contains 'one or more property-value pairs,' meaning it can have one or many. The other options are too restrictive and contradict the 'one or more' phrasing.

units.title: Text Align Property
units.summary: The CSS text-align property controls the horizontal alignment of text within an HTML element, with options including center, left, and right.
units.source_span:
The CSS `text-align` property specifies the horizontal alignment of the text in an HTML element.

```css
.h-center {
  text-align: center;
}
```

| Value  | Description                   |
| ------ | ----------------------------- |
| center | Aligns the text to the center |
| left   | Aligns the text to the left   |
| right  | Aligns the text to the right  |

explanation.text: The CSS `text-align` property controls where text lines up horizontally inside an HTML element. You can choose to align text to the center, left, or right side of its container.

analogy.text: Imagine arranging books on a shelf. The shelf itself is like the HTML element, and the books are like the text. Just as you can push all books to the left side, center them in the middle, or push them to the right side of the shelf, the text-align property pushes text to the left, center, or right within its element.
analogy.grounding_check: shelf = HTML element ('in an HTML element'), books = text ('alignment of the text'), left/center/right positions = the three alignment values (center, left, right)

scenarios.text: A website header needs the page title centered above the navigation menu. You apply `text-align: center` to the title element so it displays in the middle of the page width.

code_playground.html:
Welcome to My Site

Your destination for learning

questions.question: What does the CSS text-align property control?
questions.explanation: The source states that text-align specifies the horizontal alignment of text in an HTML element. Vertical alignment, font size, and color are controlled by different CSS properties, not text-align.

questions.question: Which text-align value centers text horizontally?
questions.explanation: The source table shows that the value 'center' aligns text to the center. 'Middle' is used for vertical alignment in other contexts, 'align-center' is not a valid text-align value, and 'horizontal' is not an alignment value.

questions.question: What happens when you set text-align to left?
options: Text spreads evenly across the element
questions.explanation: The source table states that the 'left' value aligns text to the left. The other options describe different alignment behaviors that do not match the 'left' value.

questions.question: Which of these is a valid text-align value shown in the source?
questions.explanation: The source table lists 'right' as a valid value that aligns text to the right. 'Justify', 'top', and 'bottom' are not mentioned in the source span as valid text-align values.
